items.prompt_md: Why does the reversed traversal move to a neighbor when `heights[neighbor] >= heights[current]`, instead of `<=`?
options: Because the traversal walks backward — from the ocean uphill — so it follows the reverse of the forward "flow downhill" rule
Because higher cells are always closer to the ocean
It's a typo in the reference solution; it should be `<=`
Because the grid is guaranteed to be sorted by height
items.answer: Because the traversal walks backward — from the ocean uphill — so it follows the reverse of the forward "flow downhill" rule
items.explanation_md: Water flows from X to Y forward when `heights[Y] <= heights[X]`. Walking that edge in reverse, from Y to X, means X must satisfy `heights[X] >= heights[Y]` — uphill from the ocean's perspective.

items.prompt_md: For an R × C grid, what is the total time complexity of the two boundary-seeded reverse traversals plus the intersection?
options: O(R × C)
O(R × C × log(R × C))
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: Each reverse traversal visits every cell at most once — O(R × C) — and intersecting two sets of at most R × C cells is also O(R × C), so the whole algorithm stays linear in the grid size.

items.prompt_md: Why is a single BFS seeded from EVERY rotten orange at once ("multi-source") the right approach for spreading rot, rather than looping over rotten oranges and starting a fresh traversal from each one individually?
options: Because rot spreads from all rotten oranges simultaneously each minute; seeding one BFS queue with every rotten cell at time 0 makes each BFS layer correspond exactly to one elapsed minute
Because multi-source BFS uses less memory than storing the grid itself
Because a single-source BFS cannot visit more than one cell per layer
Because oranges must be processed in a specific numeric order
items.answer: Because rot spreads from all rotten oranges simultaneously each minute; seeding one BFS queue with every rotten cell at time 0 makes each BFS layer correspond exactly to one elapsed minute
items.explanation_md: All rotten oranges age fresh neighbors at the same rate in parallel, so putting every one of them in the queue up front and expanding layer by layer naturally tracks elapsed minutes — running BFS from one rotten orange at a time would give the wrong minute count and repeat work.

items.prompt_md: In "surrounded regions," why do you first mark every 'O' cell reachable from the border as safe, before flipping any remaining 'O' to 'X'?
options: Because any 'O' connected to the border can escape and must never be captured, so marking those first tells you unambiguously that every OTHER remaining 'O' is fully enclosed
Because it's faster to process border cells first, for no functional reason
Because border cells must always stay as 'O' regardless of connectivity
Because DFS can only start from border cells
items.answer: Because any 'O' connected to the border can escape and must never be captured, so marking those first tells you unambiguously that every OTHER remaining 'O' is fully enclosed
items.explanation_md: A region of 'O's is only "surrounded" if it never touches the border; running one traversal from every border 'O' identifies exactly the non-surrounded region, leaving everything else safe to flip.

items.prompt_md: You implement multi-source BFS by marking a cell visited only when it is dequeued, not when it is enqueued. What goes wrong?
options: The same cell can be pushed onto the queue multiple times by different sources before any of them dequeues it, wasting work and inflating the queue
The traversal terminates immediately without visiting any cells
It has no effect at all — dequeue-time and enqueue-time marking are always equivalent
It causes the traversal to skip half the grid
items.answer: The same cell can be pushed onto the queue multiple times by different sources before any of them dequeues it, wasting work and inflating the queue
items.explanation_md: Several neighbors can discover the same unvisited cell before it's dequeued; marking only at dequeue lets it be enqueued once per discovering neighbor instead of once total, inflating the queue and the work done.

items.prompt_md: You forget to seed the BFS queue with the starting state for the source cells themselves (e.g., you don't mark every rotten orange as minute 0 before starting). What breaks?
options: Every downstream distance/minute count can be thrown off, since it's computed relative to a baseline that was never correctly established at the actual sources
The BFS runs faster because it has fewer starting cells
Nothing breaks, since sources are revisited automatically
The grid becomes disconnected
items.answer: Every downstream distance/minute count can be thrown off, since it's computed relative to a baseline that was never correctly established at the actual sources
items.explanation_md: Every distance downstream is computed relative to the sources' own baseline; if the sources aren't correctly seeded at distance/minute zero, every cell's computed value inherits that error.

items.prompt_md: What condition must hold for a "reverse traversal from the boundary" to be valid, instead of running the forward rule from every cell?
options: The forward adjacency check must be re-expressible from the neighbor's side — i.e., you can tell whether X can reach Y just as easily by checking the same relation from Y looking back at X
The grid must be square
Every cell must already be visited at least once before the traversal starts
The graph must be undirected
items.answer: The forward adjacency check must be re-expressible from the neighbor's side — i.e., you can tell whether X can reach Y just as easily by checking the same relation from Y looking back at X
items.explanation_md: Reversing only works when the edge test is symmetric enough to check from either endpoint — here, `heights[Y] <= heights[X]` (forward) is the same fact as `heights[X] >= heights[Y]` (checked while walking from Y to X) — so walking backward from the target visits exactly the cells that could reach it forward.

items.prompt_md: In "walls and gates," where every empty room must be filled with its distance to the nearest gate, why seed BFS from every gate at once rather than running BFS from each empty room outward to find its nearest gate?
options: One multi-source BFS from all gates reaches every room in O(R × C) total, while a BFS from each room separately would redo overlapping work for every single room
BFS can only be seeded from cells labeled as walls
Rooms cannot be visited more than once regardless of seeding
Gates must always outnumber rooms for BFS to work
items.answer: One multi-source BFS from all gates reaches every room in O(R × C) total, while a BFS from each room separately would redo overlapping work for every single room
items.explanation_md: Flooding outward from every gate simultaneously assigns every room its correct shortest distance in one linear pass, instead of repeating a full traversal per room.

items.prompt_md: For a grid with R rows and C columns, what is the time complexity of multi-source BFS seeded from all sources at once (e.g., all rotten oranges, or all gates)?
options: O(R × C)
O(R × C × log(R × C))
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: Every cell is enqueued and dequeued at most once regardless of how many sources seed the queue, so the total work stays linear in the number of cells.

items.prompt_md: If you instead ran a separate single-source BFS/DFS from EACH of the K initially rotten oranges, one at a time, what would the worst-case time complexity become?
options: O(K × R × C)
O(R × C)
O(K + R × C)
O(log K × R × C)
items.answer: O(K × R × C)
items.explanation_md: Each of the K separate traversals can visit up to the whole grid, so the work multiplies by K instead of being shared across sources in one pass — exactly what multi-source BFS avoids.

items.prompt_md: In "as far from land as possible," you must find the water cell whose distance to the nearest land cell is maximized. Why does multi-source BFS from every land cell directly give you that answer, without computing distances from each water cell individually?
options: Multi-source BFS expands outward in synchronized layers, so the last water cell reached (the final BFS layer) is, by construction, the one farthest from any land cell
Because water cells are always adjacent to land cells
Because only one land cell needs to be checked
Because BFS visits cells in order of their row index
items.answer: Multi-source BFS expands outward in synchronized layers, so the last water cell reached (the final BFS layer) is, by construction, the one farthest from any land cell
items.explanation_md: Since every land cell starts the flood at the same time, the layer number at which a water cell is first reached is exactly its distance to the nearest land — so the deepest layer reached is the maximum such distance, found in one pass.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of a multi-source BFS over an R × C grid, counting the queue and the visited/distance tracking?
options: O(R × C)
O(R + C)
O(log(R × C))
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: In the worst case the visited/distance structure and the queue can each hold up to every cell in the grid, so both stay linear in the grid's size.

items.prompt_md: A grid contains fresh oranges (1), rotten oranges (2), and empty cells (0). Every minute, each rotten orange rots any orthogonally adjacent fresh orange. You must find the minimum number of minutes until no fresh orange remains, or determine it's impossible. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every initially rotten orange at once and tracking elapsed minutes by BFS layer
DFS from a single arbitrarily chosen rotten orange, counting total fresh oranges visited
Union-Find, unioning every orange with its adjacent oranges and counting groups
Topological sort of the oranges by row then column
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every initially rotten orange at once and tracking elapsed minutes by BFS layer
items.explanation_md: Rot spreads from every rotten orange in parallel each minute, so seeding one BFS queue with all of them at once and reading elapsed minutes off the BFS layer is exactly the multi-source shape — a single DFS from one orange would miss the other sources entirely.

items.prompt_md: In a grid of 'X' and 'O' characters, you must flip every 'O' to 'X' EXCEPT those connected (up/down/left/right) to a border cell, which must stay 'O'. Which approach fits?
options: Boundary-seeded DFS/BFS starting from every border 'O', marking everything it reaches as safe, then flip every unmarked 'O'
Union-Find comparing every pair of 'O' cells in the grid to each other
Multi-source BFS seeded from every 'X' cell instead of every 'O' cell
Topological sort of the 'O' cells by distance from the center
items.answer: Boundary-seeded DFS/BFS starting from every border 'O', marking everything it reaches as safe, then flip every unmarked 'O'
items.explanation_md: Only 'O's reachable from the border can escape capture, so one traversal seeded from every border 'O' cleanly separates "safe" from "capturable" in a single linear pass.

items.prompt_md: Given a grid of 0s and 1s, you must compute, for every cell, its distance to the nearest 0 cell. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every 0 cell at distance 0 and expanding outward layer by layer
Single-source BFS from one arbitrarily chosen 0 cell
Union-Find grouping every cell with its nearest 0
Sort all cells by their distance to the top-left corner
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every 0 cell at distance 0 and expanding outward layer by layer
items.explanation_md: Seeding every 0 cell into the BFS queue at once means each cell's first-reached layer is exactly its true distance to its nearest zero — computed for the whole grid in one pass instead of one search per cell.

items.prompt_md: A grid represents a building with walls (-1), gates (0), and empty rooms (INF). You must fill every empty room with its distance to the nearest gate. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every gate cell simultaneously and propagating distances outward
DFS from each empty room individually, searching outward until a gate is found
Union-Find merging every room with its nearest gate
A monotonic stack of rooms ordered by distance to the nearest wall
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every gate cell simultaneously and propagating distances outward
items.explanation_md: Flooding outward from all gates at once assigns every room its correct shortest distance in one linear pass; searching outward from each room instead repeats overlapping work across the whole grid.

items.prompt_md: In a grid of heights, water can flow from a cell to a neighbor only if the neighbor's height is less than or equal to the current cell's height. Two oceans border opposite edges of the grid. You must find every cell from which water can reach BOTH oceans. Which approach fits?
options: Run a boundary-seeded reverse traversal from each ocean's edge cells separately, then intersect the two reachable sets
Run a single forward DFS/BFS from every cell in the grid, checking whether it reaches both oceans
Union-Find, unioning each cell with every lower-or-equal neighbor and checking which group touches both edges
Sort all cells by height and greedily assign them to whichever ocean is closer
items.answer: Run a boundary-seeded reverse traversal from each ocean's edge cells separately, then intersect the two reachable sets
items.explanation_md: Running the flow rule forward from every cell is quadratic; reversing it and flooding once from each ocean's border, then intersecting, is linear and gives the same answer.

items.prompt_md: In a grid of land (1) and water (0) cells, you must find the water cell whose distance to the nearest land cell is as large as possible (or report there is none, if the grid is all land or all water). Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS seeded from every land cell, tracking the final (deepest) layer reached
DFS from the single closest land cell to the grid's center
Union-Find merging every land cell with adjacent water cells
A monotonic stack of water cells ordered by row index
items.answer: Multi-source BFS seeded from every land cell, tracking the final (deepest) layer reached
items.explanation_md: Flooding outward from every land cell simultaneously makes each water cell's first-reached layer its true distance to the nearest land, so the last layer reached is the maximum such distance.

items.prompt_md: A maze grid has several exit cells scattered along its border. Starting from a given interior cell, you must find the minimum number of steps to reach ANY exit. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS seeded from every exit cell at once, computing each cell's distance to its nearest exit, then reading off the value at the starting cell
DFS from the starting cell, stopping at the first border cell reached regardless of distance
Union-Find merging the starting cell with every exit cell
Topological sort of the maze cells by row
items.answer: Multi-source BFS seeded from every exit cell at once, computing each cell's distance to its nearest exit, then reading off the value at the starting cell
items.explanation_md: Seeding the BFS from every exit at once computes the nearest-exit distance for the whole grid in one pass — including the starting cell — rather than searching outward from the start and hoping to hit a border cell efficiently.

items.prompt_md: You're given a weighted, directed graph (positive edge weights representing travel time) and a single source node. You must find the shortest travel time from the source to every other node. Which approach fits?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm, repeatedly expanding the closest not-yet-finalized node using a min-heap
Multi-source BFS seeded from every node with an incoming edge
Boundary-seeded reverse traversal from every node with no outgoing edges
Union-Find, unioning nodes along each edge in weight order
items.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm, repeatedly expanding the closest not-yet-finalized node using a min-heap
items.explanation_md: Plain BFS (multi-source or not) only produces correct shortest paths when every edge costs the same; once edges carry different positive weights, the frontier must be expanded in increasing distance order, which is exactly what a min-heap-driven Dijkstra provides.

items.prompt_md: You're given the head of a singly linked list and must determine whether it contains a cycle, using O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Fast/slow pointers (Floyd's cycle detection), advancing one pointer twice as fast as the other until they meet or one reaches the end
Multi-source BFS seeded from the head and every node it points to
Boundary-seeded traversal starting from the list's tail
Union-Find, unioning each node with its successor
items.answer: Fast/slow pointers (Floyd's cycle detection), advancing one pointer twice as fast as the other until they meet or one reaches the end
items.explanation_md: A singly linked list has exactly one successor per node, so the O(1)-space fast/slow pointer technique is the purpose-built tool here — there's no grid or multi-source structure to flood-fill.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and a target value, and must determine whether any two distinct elements sum to the target. Which approach fits?
options: A hash set: for each element, check whether (target - element) has already been seen
Multi-source BFS seeded from every array index
Boundary-seeded reverse traversal from the array's two ends
Union-Find merging indices whose values sum close to the target
items.answer: A hash set: for each element, check whether (target - element) has already been seen
items.explanation_md: There's no grid, graph, or connectivity structure here at all — just a single-pass complement lookup, which a hash set answers directly in O(n) without any traversal.
